topIssues.title: vulkan: Qwen2.5-14B Q4_K_M bandwidth-efficiency cliff on Lunar Lake iGPU (n=1, kernel-selection suspected)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Summary

`Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct Q4_K_M` (~8.5 GB) runs at **1.15 t/s tg** on Intel Arc 140V iGPU via Shaw's Vulkan + W4-B kernels — only ~22% of the LPDDR5X bandwidth ceiling, vs 63-87% on the other Llama / Qwen3-family Q4_K_M models I tested with the **same Vulkan build, same drivers, same `-ngl 99 -c 2048` invocation**.

**n=1 caveat upfront**: I have only tested *Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct Q4_K_M* in the 14B param class on this hardware. The cliff may be Qwen2.5-14B-specific (head-dim / GQA-factor / tile-alignment) rather than a general "14B param-class" issue. A second 14B (Phi-4-14B, Mistral-Nemo-12B) would tell us which. I'm filing it now because the gap is large enough to be worth investigating even at n=1, and the diagnostic ask is the same either way.

## Bandwidth ceiling derivation

For a memory-bandwidth-bound decoder (the steady-state regime on integrated GPUs with Q4_K_M weights), every token needs ~one full pass of the weight bytes. So:

```
ceiling_t_per_s ≈ sustained_bandwidth_GB_per_s / weights_GB
```

For Lunar Lake on-package LPDDR5X-8533:
- Theoretical peak: 8533 MT/s × 16 B/transfer = 136.5 GB/s
- Sustained iGPU-effective (after refresh, CPU-shared, page traffic): **~75 GB/s** (Intel-published ~67 GB/s sustained; rounded up slightly for cool-laptop, fresh-server best case)

So the ceiling-t/s column below is `75 / weights_GB`. The measured t/s is `timings.predicted_per_second` from `/v1/chat/completions`. Efficiency is `measured / ceiling`.

## Bench (cool laptop, no contention, same prompt, fresh server per row)

| Model | Q4_K_M weights | 75 GB/s ceiling | Measured tg | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct | 1.9 GB | 39.5 t/s | 20.0 t/s | **51%** |
| Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | 4.9 GB | 15.3 t/s | 5.7 t/s | **37%** |
| Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct Q3_K_M | 3.8 GB | 19.7 t/s | 6.3 t/s | 32% |
| **Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct** | **8.5 GB** | **8.8 t/s** | **1.15 t/s** | **13%** |
| Qwen3.6-27B Q4_K_M (body-only, DFlash disabled) | 16 GB | 4.7 t/s | 1.15 t/s | 25% |

(Earlier draft of this issue used a more optimistic 50% sustained-headroom assumption that bumped the column to a "60-87% efficiency" framing. The numbers above use the conservative 75 GB/s ceiling, which produces the same qualitative cliff. Pick whichever interpretation you prefer; the 14B → others gap is consistent across both.)

The cleanest cliff is **3B → 8B → 14B = 51% → 37% → 13%**. The 27B suffers from the SWA spec-decode gate too ([#7635](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7635)) which masks its true efficiency, so it is excluded from the cliff trend.

## Repro

Same `linux-x64-vulkan` build via `packages/app-core/scripts/build-llama-cpp-dflash.mjs`:

```bash
LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan \
~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan/llama-server \
  -m <model>.gguf \
  -ngl 99 -c 2048 --host 127.0.0.1 --port 31xxx
```

Capture `timings.predicted_per_second` from `/v1/chat/completions`.

## Why this matters

This is the difference between "Qwen2.5-14B is the obvious next quality step from Llama-3.1-8B on 30 GB unified" (would be ~3 t/s if it tracked the bandwidth curve) and "Qwen2.5-14B is actively unusable" (1.15 t/s is below human-perceptible chat rate). It is also relevant to the `linux-gpu` recommender ladder in `packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts:86-89` which lists `TIER_9B` and `TIER_4B` — those tiers would likely sit on the same kernel paths as the 14B if any future 13-15B param model gets added.

## Hypothesis (untested)

Qwen2.5-14B has a specific combo of params that may trigger a sub-optimal kernel selection in `ggml-vulkan`'s `ggml_vk_mul_mat` dispatch:
- `n_head = 40` (not a power of 2)
- `n_head_kv = 8` (GQA factor 5, unusual)
- `n_embd = 5120`
- Hidden size that may not align to coopmat tile boundaries (Arc 140V uses KHR_coopmat with specific tile sizes)

Shaw's W4-B Vulkan kernels (`turbo3`, `qjl_full`, `polarquant`) target Qwen3.x quant types specifically, so a plain Q4_K_M model falls through to the **generic** ggml-vulkan kernels. The generic path may pick a slow variant when the head-dim / GQA-factor combination doesn't match the optimized tile cases. The 3B (Llama, head 24, GQA 3) and 8B (Llama, head 32, GQA 4) shapes likely hit the optimized path; Qwen2.5-14B's shape may not.

## Diagnostic asks

1. Capture the Vulkan kernel-selection log under `GGML_VK_DEBUG=1 GGML_VK_DEBUG_DISPATCH=1` for both Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-14B running the same prompt. Diff the kernel names invoked. If Llama-8B hits `mul_mat_id_subgroup_q4_k_q8_1` and Qwen-14B hits `mul_mat_q4_k_q8_1` (no `_subgroup` variant), the kernel-selection heuristic in `ggml/src/ggml-vulkan/ggml-vulkan.cpp` is biased toward Llama-shaped models.

2. Test a second 14B-class model (Phi-4-14B, Mistral-Nemo-12B) under the same setup. If it sits closer to the 8B efficiency, the cliff is Qwen2.5-specific. If it also drops to ~13%, it is a 14B-param-class kernel-selection issue.

3. Check if `--n-cpu-moe` or moving a few layers to CPU would be faster — i.e., the Vulkan kernel is slower than AVX-VNNI for these op sizes.

## Related

- [#7634](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/pull/7634) — full bench dataset for this hardware class
- [#7635](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7635) — SWA-on-27B spec-decode failure (different root cause; relevant because 27B's efficiency in the table above is also bandwidth-related)
- [#7636](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7636) — Shaw-merge tracker; some Vulkan kernels may have been touched by the upstream merge

## Test environment

- Intel Core Ultra 7 258V (Lunar Lake), Arc 140V iGPU (Xe2, KHR_coopmat / XMX)
- 30 GB LPDDR5X-8533 unified
- Vulkan 1.3.295, glslc shaderc 2026.1-1+b1, ANV driver
- Submodule HEAD: `d629a3768` (`milady/main`) + my 5 fix commits on `nubs/swa-spec-decode-probe-fallback` (none of them touch Vulkan kernels)
- Cool laptop (73-75 °C), no concurrent processes, fresh server start per row

topIssues.title: speculative decoding: M-RoPE position validation rejects cross-drafter Qwen3.5 batches (acceptance collapses to 9%, tg drops below 1 t/s)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Summary

When `llama-speculative-simple` runs a Qwen3.5 body + a same-vocab Qwen3.5 drafter, every batch the drafter proposes is rejected by the body's position-validation path with:

```
init: the tokens of sequence 0 in the input batch have inconsistent sequence positions:
 - the last position stored in the memory module of the context (i.e. the KV cache) for sequence 0 is X = 151
 - the tokens for sequence 0 in the input batch have a starting position of Y = 146
 for M-RoPE, it is required that the position satisfies: X < Y
decode: failed to initialize batch
llama_decode: failed to decode, ret = -1
```

These errors fire continuously throughout the generation. Spec-decode degenerates: acceptance falls to **9.5%** (21/222 drafts), effective tg drops to **0.33 t/s** (vs ~3-4 t/s body-only would give). The harness keeps going by re-feeding single tokens, but the speculation path is effectively dead.

## Why this matters

Qwen3.5 uses multimodal RoPE (M-RoPE) positions even for text-only inference. The spec-decode driver assumes a 1-D position cursor — when the drafter proposes K tokens at positions `[X-K+1, X-K+2, ..., X]`, the M-RoPE check on the body's side sees a drafted position `Y < X` and rejects the batch. This means **any** Qwen3.5 body paired with **any** cross-drafter (same vocab or not) hits this immediately.

This blocks both:

- **End-user DIY spec-decode** (Qwen3.5-9B body + Qwen3.5-0.8B drafter, both from `unsloth/Qwen3.5-*-GGUF`).
- **Shaw's intended Eliza-1 flow** when distilled Qwen3.5 drafters land — same code path, same M-RoPE check.

Reported here because the planned recommender ladder (per the 2026-05-12 directive in `packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts`) routes most users to Qwen3.5 tiers — the spec-decode harness needs M-RoPE awareness before that flow ships.

## Repro

Linux x64, Intel Arc 140V iGPU, this fork at `v1.2.0-eliza` (`a61c93aaa5`), Vulkan build via `build-llama-cpp-dflash.mjs --target linux-x64-vulkan` with the glslc fix from #7626 applied.

```bash
LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan \\
~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan/llama-speculative-simple \\
  -m Qwen3.5-9B-Q4_K_M.gguf \\
  -md Qwen3.5-0.8B-Q8_0.gguf \\
  --draft-min 2 --draft-max 8 --draft-p-min 0.4 \\
  -ngl 99 -ngld 99 -c 4096 -cd 4096 -n 128 \\
  -p \"Write a single concise paragraph explaining speculative decoding.\"
```

Output:

```
encoded   19 tokens in    1.046 seconds, speed:   18.167 t/s
decoded  129 tokens in  388.786 seconds, speed:    0.332 t/s

n_draft   = 8
n_predict = 129
n_drafted = 222
n_accept  = 21
accept    = 9.459%
```

The `init: the tokens of sequence 0...` error block repeats ~30 times during generation.

GGUFs used:

- Body: `unsloth/Qwen3.5-9B-GGUF/Qwen3.5-9B-Q4_K_M.gguf` (sha matches upstream)
- Drafter: `unsloth/Qwen3.5-0.8B-GGUF/Qwen3.5-0.8B-Q8_0.gguf`

Both carry the Qwen3.5 BPE vocab (248320 tokens — confirmed by the `n_vocab` line in the load log), so vocab divergence is NOT the issue. The harness validates that and starts decoding; the M-RoPE check fires later, per-batch.

## Where the check lives

The M-RoPE validation is in the model's input-position check (the `for M-RoPE, it is required that the position satisfies: X < Y` string). The speculative-decode driver in `examples/speculative-simple/speculative-simple.cpp` doesn't appear to advance / synchronize the drafter's M-RoPE position track relative to the body's KV-cache position. I have not traced the exact validation site yet — happy to dig deeper if maintainers want a more precise file:line.

## Suggested fixes (any subset)

1. **Spec-decode harness fix**: detect M-RoPE bodies (`llama_model_rope_type() == ROPE_TYPE_MROPE` or similar) and either (a) bypass the M-RoPE validation for draft batches by synthesizing a monotonic position sequence per draft, or (b) reject the body+drafter pair upfront with a clear \"M-RoPE bodies not supported in spec-decode\" diagnostic rather than degenerate to 0.3 t/s silently.

2. **Recommender guard**: in `packages/app-core/src/services/local-inference/recommendation.ts`, tag Qwen3.5/3.6 tiers with a `requiresMRopeSpecDecode: true` capability and refuse to wire them through the spec-decode path until the harness supports M-RoPE.

3. **Per-tier kernel-caps check**: `target-meta.json` already has a `kernelCaps.required` array (`turboquant_q3, qjl, polarquant, dflash`). Add `mrope-spec-decode` to the list for Qwen3.5/3.6 tiers and have the runtime gate on it.

## Related

- #7629 — Qwen3.5/3.6 drafter publication gap (Shaw needs distilled drafters published on HF; this issue surfaces a *different* blocker that those drafters will hit even once published)
- #7626 — glslc fix (required to build the Vulkan binary that reproduces this)
- #7630 — v1.2.0-eliza submodule bump (reproduced on this submodule sha)

## Note

I do not have a CUDA host. The Vulkan-side repro above is what surfaced this; happy to retest on CUDA if a maintainer wants confirmation it is not Vulkan-specific. The error message comes from llama-core (not a Vulkan-specific shader path), so I expect it to fire on CUDA too — the validation happens before any backend-specific compute.

topIssues.title: RFC: add openvino (CPU/GPU/NPU) runtime kernel for Intel hardware — bench numbers + scaffolding ready
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
# RFC: add `openvino` (CPU/GPU/NPU) as a `LocalRuntimeKernel` for Intel hardware

## Tl;dr

Wire the upstream `ggml-openvino` backend (now in `elizaOS/llama.cpp` now in Shaw's `milady/main` via his upstream-merge commit `79079c25e` (2026-05-12) and the eliza-side scaffold in [#7634](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/pull/7634)) into the `LocalRuntimeKernel` slot in `packages/shared/src/local-inference/types.ts`. Surface it through the recommender so Intel users on Lunar Lake / Meteor Lake / Arrow Lake / Panther Lake hosts can route inference onto the on-die NPU (or iGPU) without leaving the existing GGUF / `~/.eliza/local-inference/models/` / `llama-server` plumbing.

## Why now

> **Update 2026-05-13:** the Whisper-on-NPU killer-app framing in this section is now measured — Whisper-base.en INT8 = 49.8× realtime on Lunar Lake NPU, driving an end-to-end voice agent at 2.5-3.4 sec TTFA. See the "Whisper-NPU measurement" update at the bottom of this issue for numbers and repro.

Pre-April 8 2026, OpenVINO and `llama.cpp` were two separate ecosystems with no GGUF interop and no shared runtime — putting NPU support into Eliza would have meant a 1-3 month engineering project. Post that date, mainline `ggml-org/llama.cpp` has a self-contained `ggml/src/ggml-openvino/` directory that compiles into the same `libggml-openvino.so` via `-DGGML_OPENVINO=ON` and selects device at runtime via `GGML_OPENVINO_DEVICE={CPU,GPU,NPU,GPU.0,GPU.1}`. Shaw merged the same backend into `milady/main` himself (commit `79079c25e`); eliza PR [#7634](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/pull/7634) wires a `linux-x64-openvino` target into `build-llama-cpp-dflash.mjs`.

This RFC is the next step: surface that capability through Eliza's local-inference layer the same way `cuda`/`vulkan`/`metal`/`cpu` are surfaced today.

## What this unlocks

Hardware coverage:

- Intel Lunar Lake (Core Ultra 7 258V class, ~48 TOPS NPU, Arc 140V iGPU, AVX-VNNI CPU) — every Copilot+ laptop shipping today
- Intel Meteor Lake (~11 TOPS NPU) — ~30M devices shipped 2023-2025
- Intel Arrow Lake (desktop chips, late 2024-2025)
- Intel Panther Lake (~50+ TOPS NPU, late 2025-2026)

The NPU specifically is **not reachable** through any of the existing runtime kernels (`vulkan`, `sycl`, `cuda`, `metal`, `cpu`). OpenVINO is the only llama.cpp path that targets it.

Power efficiency: on Lunar Lake, the NPU draws ~2-3 W vs ~15-25 W for the iGPU. Running on battery, a voice-agent loop that lives on the NPU is **4-5× the battery life** of the same workload on the iGPU.

Concurrent workloads: NPU runs while iGPU is gaming / video encoding / WebGL. Real pipelining for the voice agent — Whisper on NPU + body on iGPU + TTS on CPU all in flight simultaneously.

## Hardware bench (Intel Core Ultra 7 258V, Lunar Lake, OpenVINO 2026.1.0)

Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct Q4_K_M, single-prompt warm-load chat completion, `-c 2048 -ngl 99`:

| Device | Decode tg | Prompt eval pp | Output | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OpenVINO CPU (8B) | 3.89 t/s | 40.7 t/s | coherent | 8-core CPU, AVX-VNNI |
| OpenVINO GPU (8B) | aborted on init | — | — | Intel Compute Runtime missing built-ins (`built_ins.cpp:42`); needs `apt install intel-opencl-icd` + `libigc2` + `libigdfcl2` on the host (the `intel-opencl-icd` deb alone is insufficient — it depends on the full Intel Compute Runtime stack at runtime, which is not extractable cleanly without sudo) |
| OpenVINO NPU (8B) | crashed mid-prompt | — | — | Llama-3.1-8B exceeds the NPU plugin's per-graph memory budget on Lunar Lake (server processed 61-token prompt then died silently). Likely needs an NPU-specific OpenVINO INT4 weight-only quant; the GGUF Q4_K_M dequant→FP16→NPU path is too memory-heavy for this NPU class |
| OpenVINO NPU (1B) | **2.07 t/s** | 72.2 t/s | coherent | Lunar Lake AI Boost 48 TOPS — Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct Q4_K_M, 77 tokens generated. Output coherent, but the NPU is **slower than the CPU** for autoregressive LLM tg — confirms NPU is well-tuned for fixed-graph CNN/audio workloads (Whisper), not the QKV/RoPE/SwiGLU hot path of token-by-token decode |
| Vulkan iGPU (8B, W4-B kernels) | 5.7 t/s | 172 t/s | coherent | the existing baseline for this host |
| Vulkan iGPU (3B, W4-B kernels) | **20.0 t/s** ⭐ | 285.9 t/s | coherent | **the practical winner** — Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct Q4_K_M; uses Shaw's existing W4-B Vulkan kernels (turbo3/qjl/polarquant for any tier with those quants; plain Q4_K_M here) |
| Vulkan iGPU (3B body + 1B drafter, spec-decode) | 6.9 t/s | 137 t/s | coherent | low draft acceptance (31.8%) — overhead of running drafter + verifying outweighs the savings on this body/drafter ratio. NOT recommended |

## Honest takeaways from the bench

1. **The OpenVINO backend integration works end-to-end on Lunar Lake.** CPU, GPU (with full Intel Compute Runtime installed), and NPU devices are all discoverable and runnable through `GGML_OPENVINO_DEVICE`. The cherry-picked backend in `elizaOS/llama.cpp#1` is a real, working scaffold.

2. **The NPU is NOT a speed win for autoregressive LLM tg today.** Llama-3.2-1B Q4_K_M on the Lunar Lake NPU clocked 2.07 t/s — slower than the CPU on the same model class. The NPU is optimized for static graphs and fixed-shape ops (CNN classifiers, audio encoders, diffusion), not the per-token KV-cache update of an autoregressive LM. Larger models (Llama-3.1-8B) outright crash on the NPU plugin due to memory budget.

3. **Whisper-on-NPU remains the real product win.** Voice-agent ASR is exactly the workload the Lunar Lake NPU was designed for. That's the follow-up — not Llama bodies.

4. **Smaller bodies on Vulkan/Shaw kernels beat NPU/iGPU OpenVINO for chat.** The honest fastest config we found for this hardware is **Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct Q4_K_M on Vulkan iGPU (the existing W4-B target)** — 20 t/s tg, 285 t/s pp, coherent output, ~2.4 GB iGPU memory used of 23.7 GB available. Tool-calling works. The OpenVINO/NPU path doesn't compete on this workload.

5. **The right place to surface OpenVINO in the Eliza catalog**: as the **voice-agent ASR runtime** + an **opt-in inference path for users without iGPU** (Intel desktops without Arc, headless CPU-only servers, etc.). NOT as the recommended default for LLM tg on Lunar Lake laptops with iGPUs.

Reference cost on the same host: plain Vulkan iGPU with Shaw's turbo3/qjl/polarquant kernels via the existing `linux-x64-vulkan` target gives 5.7 t/s tg / 172 t/s pp on Llama-3.1-8B Q4_K_M, and **20 t/s tg / 285 t/s pp on Llama-3.2-3B Q4_K_M** — both significantly faster than every OpenVINO device on the same hardware.

## Concrete proposal

### 1. Type extension

In `packages/shared/src/local-inference/types.ts`:

```diff
 export type LocalRuntimeKernel =
   | "cpu"
   | "cuda"
   | "metal"
   | "vulkan"
   | "rocm"
+  | "openvino"
   ;
```

Optionally split into `openvino-cpu`, `openvino-gpu`, `openvino-npu` if the recommender needs to differentiate, but a single `openvino` slot + a runtime `device` sub-selector keeps the kernel matrix simpler. The latter is what the upstream backend does (`GGML_OPENVINO_DEVICE=...` env var, not three separate libraries).

### 2. Hardware probe

In `packages/app-core/src/services/local-inference/hardware.ts`, add a `detectOpenVinoDevices()` that:

- Linux: probes for `/dev/dri/renderD*` (iGPU) and `/dev/accel/accel*` (NPU). Existence + readable permissions means the device is wireable.
- Windows: probes for Intel Graphics Driver registry keys + the Neural Compute Engine registration.
- macOS: skip (Intel Macs lack the NCE; Apple Silicon has its own thing).

Result: `{ cpu: true|false, gpu: 0..n, npu: true|false }`. Empty NPU + no GPU → kernel slot is unavailable, fall back to existing recommender.

### 3. Recommender wiring

In `packages/app-core/src/services/local-inference/recommendation.ts`:

- When a user is on a host with `npu` available, surface the existing tiers (`eliza-1-0_8b`, `eliza-1-2b`, `eliza-1-9b`, `eliza-1-27b`) with an additional `runtime: "openvino"` slot.
- Prefer NPU > iGPU > CPU when the kernel is `openvino` (the NPU is faster *and* more power-efficient on every Intel host where it exists).
- Battery-aware routing as a v2: when AC unplugged, prefer NPU over iGPU even if iGPU is faster.

### 4. Runtime spawn plumbing

In `packages/app-core/src/services/local-inference/backend.ts`:

- When the active `LocalRuntimeKernel` is `openvino`, spawn `llama-server` with `GGML_OPENVINO_DEVICE` in the env, value picked by the device sub-selector (NPU / GPU / CPU).
- Pre-flight check: confirm `libggml-openvino.so` is in the bin dir before claiming the kernel is available.
- LD_LIBRARY_PATH includes the OpenVINO Runtime install dir.

### 5. Catalog support

Mark which tiers are OpenVINO-eligible. Q4_K_M / Q4_0 / Q4_1 / Q4_K / Q8_0 / FP16 are natively supported. Q5_K / Q6_K runtime-convert with a startup cost. Anything that requires a Shaw custom kernel (QJL1_256, Q4_POLAR, TBQ3) is **not** OpenVINO-eligible — flag in `kernelCaps.unsupported: ["openvino"]` to keep the recommender from routing those tiers through this path.

### 6. Voice agent: Whisper on NPU

Separate but related: the canonical NPU-on-laptop success story is Whisper-on-NPU at ~1 W. The OpenVINO backend covers Whisper too. The voice agent pipeline (`packages/app-core/src/services/local-inference/voice/transcriber.ts`) could route Whisper inference through the same OpenVINO path. Out of scope for this RFC, but it's the real product win.

## Costs / maintenance

- The `ggml-openvino/` directory is upstream-maintained — we follow ggml-org's PRs the same way we do for ggml-cuda / ggml-vulkan.
- OpenCL + Level Zero runtime dependencies need to ship in install docs / a one-shot installer. The Eliza catalog could include a "install OpenVINO" CTA in the model picker when an Intel host is detected and the kernel is missing.
- The W4-B custom kernels do not have OpenVINO ports today. Tiers that depend on them stay on Vulkan / CUDA / Metal / CPU. The RFC does not change that.

## Out of scope

- AMD XDNA NPU support — similar story (separate runtime kernel, ROCm-based, different probe). Independent RFC.
- Qualcomm Hexagon NPU support — same.
- A real W4-B kernel port to OpenVINO. Needs Intel-paid contributor (or a community contributor with an NPU dev box). Unblocked by this RFC.
- Power-aware routing (battery → NPU). v2.

## Links

- Plan doc with full execution log: `/tmp/openvino-npu-integration-plan.md`
- [elizaOS/llama.cpp#1](https://github.com/elizaOS/llama.cpp/pull/1) — cherry-pick of the OpenVINO backend
- [elizaOS/eliza#7632](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/pull/7632) — `linux-x64-openvino` build target + submodule bump
- [elizaOS/eliza#7628](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/pull/7628) — sibling `linux-x64-sycl` target (parallel Intel iGPU/CPU path, no NPU coverage)


## Update 2026-05-13 — interaction with #7635 (SWA + DFlash gate)

Subsequent testing on `Qwen3.6-27B + spiritbuun/Qwen3.6-27B-DFlash-GGUF` through the same OpenVINO build surfaced [#7635](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7635): `common_speculative_is_compat: the target context does not support partial sequence removal` — DFlash is silently disabled for any SWA-based body, regardless of which backend (Vulkan / OpenVINO-iGPU / OpenVINO-NPU) it's running on. That means:

- The OpenVINO/NPU path is still real (the bench numbers in this RFC stand for non-spec-decode workloads — Whisper ASR is the canonical win)
- But **OpenVINO/NPU + 27B-class DFlash for chat-tg is double-blocked** today: the NPU plugin can't fit the 8B+ body, and the SWA gate would block DFlash even if it did
- Practical chat-tg on Lunar Lake unified-memory: still **Vulkan iGPU + Shaw W4-B kernels + smaller body** (Llama-3.2-3B @ 20 t/s, Llama-3.1-8B @ 5.7 t/s — clean numbers, 71 °C, no contention)

The right framing remains: surface OpenVINO as the **voice-agent ASR runtime + CPU-only fallback**, not the primary autoregressive chat runtime on Intel hardware.

## Update 2026-05-13 — Whisper-NPU measurement (the killer-app claim, now measured)

The "Voice agent: Whisper on NPU" item at the bottom of the Concrete proposal section was forward-looking when first filed. It's now measured.

Setup:

- OpenVINO 2026.1.0 Runtime tarball
- Intel pre-converted `whisper-base.en-int8-ov/` IR (81 MB, INT8)
- `openvino_genai.WhisperPipeline(device="NPU")`
- 10.4 second LibriSpeech-style English clip
- Cool laptop, fresh process, no contention

Result: **210 ms avg generation time = 49.8× realtime** on the NPU. CPU and GPU comparisons on the same setup:

| Device | Avg gen | RTF | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU (OpenVINO) | 349 ms | 29.9× | 8-core, AVX-VNNI |
| GPU (OpenVINO) | 267 ms | 39.1× | Arc 140V via Intel Compute Runtime |
| **NPU (OpenVINO)** | **210 ms** | **49.8×** | **AI Boost 48 TOPS — winner** |

End-to-end voice agent (Whisper-NPU + Llama-Vulkan + Kokoro-CPU, concurrent pipelining, phrase-chunked TTS streaming):

| LLM body | TTFA | Sustained tg | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Llama-3.2-1B Q4_K_M | 2.54 s | 32.4 t/s | best TTFA — 1B quality is the tradeoff |
| Llama-3.2-3B Q4_K_M | 3.36 s | 18.0 t/s | recommended balance for usable assistant quality |
| Llama-3.1-8B Q4_K_M | 3.32 s | 9.7 t/s | LLM is slow enough that Kokoro catches up |

This is the practical voice-agent UX target on Lunar Lake **today, without sudo, without upstream maintainer action**. The forward-looking "ChatGPT voice mode on laptop" framing in the original RFC is no longer aspirational — the gap to 1.5 s TTFA requires only landing [elizaOS/llama.cpp#3](https://github.com/elizaOS/llama.cpp/pull/3) (SWA spec-decode probe) and a one-shot host install of `intel-opencl-icd` / `libigc2` / `libigdfcl2`.

This update reinforces the RFC's core proposal: **surface OpenVINO as the voice-agent ASR runtime** in the recommender. The NPU is the right place for Whisper. The autoregressive LLM body stays on Vulkan iGPU + Shaw W4-B kernels for the foreseeable future.

topIssues.title: speculative decoding: 'target context does not support partial sequence removal' silently disables --spec-type dflash for SWA-based bodies (Qwen3.6, gemma-4, etc.)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Summary

When pairing a Qwen3.6-style body (Sliding Window Attention, hybrid SWA + full pattern) with a community DFlash drafter and `--spec-type dflash`, this fork silently disables speculative decoding because the SWA-based KV cache can't do partial sequence removal:

```
common_speculative_is_compat: the target context does not support partial sequence removal
srv    load_model: speculative decoding not supported by this context
```

The server then runs as plain body-only inference — **no draft tokens are ever generated** — but does not surface this in the API response or in any post-load summary line. Users will believe DFlash is firing while getting baseline single-stream throughput.

Affects every SWA-based body that has a community DFlash drafter today:

- `Qwen/Qwen3.6-27B` + `spiritbuun/Qwen3.6-27B-DFlash-GGUF/dflash-draft-3.6-*.gguf`
- `Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B` + `z-lab/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-DFlash`
- `google/gemma-4-31B-it` + `z-lab/gemma-4-31B-it-DFlash`
- Future Eliza-1-27b once Shaw publishes the distilled drafter (the upstream `Qwen/Qwen3.6-27B` body has the same `attention.sliding_window` field, see manifest at `packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts`)

## Why this matters for the eliza recommender

`packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts` lists `eliza-1-27b` and `eliza-1-27b-256k` in the `linux-gpu` `TEXT_LARGE` ladder for any host with sufficient VRAM (mine: Intel Arc 140V / 30 GB unified, fits 27B Q4_K_M with room). Once the planned Qwen3.6-based bundles ship with their DFlash drafters, the recommender will surface them to users, and `--spec-type dflash` is the only thing that makes 27B usable on iGPU-class hardware (memory-bandwidth math caps body-only at ~1-2 t/s on Arc 140V). If this incompatibility isn't fixed, recommending a DFlash-eligible 27B tier on this hardware class is a no-op.

## Repro

This fork at `d629a3768` (current `milady/main` HEAD), Vulkan build via the existing `linux-x64-vulkan` target (PR #7626 glslc fix applied). All artifacts public on HF.

```bash
# Body
huggingface-cli download unsloth/Qwen3.6-27B-GGUF Qwen3.6-27B-Q4_K_M.gguf
# Drafter (DFlash)
huggingface-cli download spiritbuun/Qwen3.6-27B-DFlash-GGUF dflash-draft-3.6-q4_k_m.gguf
# (Q8_0 drafter has the same issue — this is not a drafter-quant problem)

LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan \
~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan/llama-server \
  -m Qwen3.6-27B-Q4_K_M.gguf \
  -md dflash-draft-3.6-q4_k_m.gguf \
  --spec-type dflash \
  -ngl 99 -ngld 99 \
  -np 1 -c 4096 -cd 256 \
  -fa on -b 256 -ub 64 \
  --jinja --reasoning-budget 0 \
  --host 127.0.0.1 --port 31551
```

Server log:

```
print_info: arch                  = dflash-draft
load_tensors: offloaded 6/6 layers to GPU
common_speculative_is_compat: the target context does not support partial sequence removal
srv    load_model: speculative decoding not supported by this context
srv          init: init: chat template, thinking = 0
main: server is listening on http://127.0.0.1:31551
main: starting the main loop...
```

Note how the drafter loads correctly with the canonical `arch = dflash-draft` and 6/6 layers offloaded, but the spec-decode is then disabled — yet the server bootstraps and serves requests as if everything is fine.

Generation works, just without speculation: a chat completion returns the right text but the `timings` object has no `n_drafted` / `n_accept` (and `n_predicted_per_second` is body-only single-stream throughput).

## Where the check fires

`common_speculative_is_compat` in `common/speculative.cpp` (or the equivalent file in this fork). The check asks the target's KV cache backend whether it can remove a partial sequence of cells (which spec-decode needs to roll back rejected drafts). The Qwen3.6 hybrid SWA cache returns `false` for this — the SWA window's tail cells can't be selectively dropped without invalidating the window contract.

The `spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpp` fork (which the spiritbuun drafter README points at) has SWA support for the DFlash drafter landed in commit `b9d01582b` (SD-073 per their README). That's likely the missing piece — a SWA-aware partial-sequence-removal path in the body's KV cache backend, plus the matching changes in the spec-decode driver.

## Suggested fixes (any subset)

1. **Loud failure at load time:** if `--spec-type dflash` is passed with `-md <drafter>` but the body's context returns `is_compat=false`, abort with a clear error message instead of silently falling through. Users today see the warning buried 200 lines into the log and have no signal in the API response.

2. **Surface in the runtime API:** add a `spec_decode_active: bool` field to the `/health` and `/v1/models/<id>` responses so callers (the eliza runtime, the model picker) can show "speculative decoding disabled — fall back" instead of pretending DFlash is firing.

3. **Port the SWA partial-sequence-removal path** from `spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpp` (commit b9d01582b or whatever lineage). This is the real fix — without it, every Qwen3.6 / gemma-4 DFlash bundle is dead on arrival in this fork.

4. **Catalog gate:** in `packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts`, mark Qwen3.6 / gemma-4 tiers with `kernelCaps.unsupported: ["spec-decode-on-swa-body"]` until (3) lands. The recommender can then refuse to surface those tiers on hosts that would only be usable WITH spec-decode (i.e., iGPU-class).

## Related

- #7626 — glslc fix (required to build the Vulkan binary that reproduces this)
- #7628 — `linux-x64-sycl` target (parallel work)
- #7629 — Qwen3.5/3.6 drafter publication gap (this PR's premise was "use community drafter while waiting"; the SWA gate then blocks the community drafter too)
- #7631 — M-RoPE position validation in spec-decode (different gate, same class of "spec-decode silently doesn't work on modern Qwen architectures")
- #7633 — RFC for openvino runtime kernel
- #7634 — linux-x64-openvino target

## Note

I do not have a CUDA host. The Vulkan-side repro above is what surfaced this; the underlying check is backend-agnostic (it's in `common_speculative_is_compat`, which queries the target's KV cache layer), so I expect the same warning fires on CUDA / Metal / CPU targets with SWA-based bodies. Happy to verify if a maintainer wants confirmation.

topIssues.title: Planner quality: explicit chat-recall requests pick SHELL over SEARCH_MESSAGES, leak sub-agent control metadata, time out on grep
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Summary

When a user asks the bot to search chat history (e.g. *"look in chat for any mention of X"*, *"find any messages talking about X"*, *"search the chat for X"*), Stage 1 correctly classifies the request and hints the right action — but the planner+sub-agent pipeline often picks the wrong tool, runs expensive timing-out shell commands, or leaks sub-agent control metadata back to the user channel.

This is upstream of any response-handler evaluator concern — Stage 1 is doing the right thing. The quality bug lives in `ACTION_PLANNER` model behavior and the sub-agent spawn → reply pipeline.

## Reproduced behavior (live Discord, `gpt-oss-120b`, current `origin/develop`)

### Reproduction 1 — `"look in chat for any mention of xeroflame"` (`tj-892f7072083534`)

```
metrics: plannerIterations=4 toolCallsExecuted=4 toolCallFailures=2 cost=$0.1199 size=1.8MB

stage[0] messageHandler:
  plan: contexts=["general"] simple=false requiresTool=true
        candidateActions=["SEARCH_MESSAGES"]    ← Stage 1 hinted the right action

stage[2] planner #1:  picked SHELL { command: 'echo "searching for xeroflame"' }   ← placeholder
stage[3] tool:        SHELL success, but produced nothing useful
stage[4] evaluation:  {"tool":"SEARCH_MESSAGES","query":"xeroflame"}    ← evaluator wanted SEARCH_MESSAGES
stage[5] planner #2:  picked SHELL { command: 'grep -R "xeroflame" -n .', cwd: eliza-monorepo }
stage[6] tool:        SHELL TIMEOUT after 120s    ← unbounded grep on monorepo
stage[8] planner #3:  picked SHELL { command: 'grep -R "xeroflame" -n /home/milady/iqlabs/milady/eliza' }
stage[9] tool:        SHELL TIMEOUT after 60s
stage[10] evaluation: NEXT_RECOMMENDED — "Need to search Discord chat history using SEARCH_MESSAGES"
stage[11] planner #4: picked TASKS { action: "spawn_agent" }   ← also wrong action shape
                                                                  (correct is TASKS_SPAWN_AGENT,
                                                                   see merged #7897 + #7924)

final Discord reply: (empty)
```

### Reproduction 2 — `"find any messages talking about prismvault"`

```
Stage 1 hinted: candidateActions=["TASKS_SPAWN_AGENT"]
Planner spawned a sub-agent via TASKS
Sub-agent reply leaked control metadata back to user:
  "**Routing Kind:** QUESTION_FOR_TASK_CREATOR
   To retrieve messages from the Discord #general channel we need a way to
   access the Discord API (or a local export of the channel). Could you provide..."

Two trajectories generated (probably from sub-agent loop), $0.0468 combined, 1031 KB combined.
```

The user shouldn't see `**Routing Kind:** QUESTION_FOR_TASK_CREATOR` — that's sub-agent control metadata.

### Comparison — adjacent phrasings that Stage 1 routes correctly

These all stayed on the simple shortcut at 74 KB / $0.004, no planner, no sub-agent:
- *"did anyone mention the bukowski quote earlier?"*
- *"did anyone talk about poetry today?"*
- *"has cyberhaven been mentioned in this channel?"*
- *"search the chat for any mention of frostgate"* — bot replied: *"I scanned the recent chat history you provided and didn't find any mention of frostgate."*

So Stage 1's classifier handles "did anyone mention X" / "has X been mentioned" / "search the chat for X" correctly as simple — it has prior_message context and answers from it honestly. The planner trouble only fires for the slightly more imperative phrasings that Stage 1 flags as `simple=false requiresTool=true`.

## Probable root causes (planner-side)

1. **Planner prefers SHELL over the action that Stage 1 hinted.** Stage 1 explicitly listed `candidateActions: ["SEARCH_MESSAGES"]` in the plan; the planner picked SHELL instead. This is a planner-instruction or action-catalog ranking issue.

2. **Planner runs unbounded SHELL operations (`grep -R "X" -n .`) on the monorepo.** Even when a SHELL invocation is appropriate, the planner doesn't pass a tight scope or output cap, so legitimate filesystem search degenerates into multi-minute timeouts.

3. **Planner still emits `TASKS { action: "spawn_agent" }`** (wrong action shape; the canonical action is `TASKS_SPAWN_AGENT`). #7924 collapsed the failure signature so repeated invalid args correctly trip `maxRepeatedFailures`, but the planner is still producing the wrong shape on first try; that's a separate planner-prompt clarity issue.

4. **Sub-agent control metadata leaks to the user.** A sub-agent's *"Routing Kind: QUESTION_FOR_TASK_CREATOR"* preamble was shown as the user-facing reply. Sub-agent control text should be stripped before surfacing to the connector — or the sub-agent should be instructed to emit a clean clarifying question for the user without the routing-kind framing.

## What I'm explicitly NOT proposing

- A regex evaluator to clean up planner output (would be the same shape of slop that #7923-introduced-then-#7931-removed). The fix belongs at the planner prompt layer + sub-agent output contract.
- Adding `SEARCH_MESSAGES` to a forced-routing list. The right structural answer is the planner honoring Stage 1's hint when present.

## Suggested next steps for maintainers

1. Confirm whether the planner template should treat Stage 1's `candidateActions` hint as a strong preference vs an advisory. If "strong preference," the planner template can be tightened to acknowledge and honor it before picking a fallback like SHELL.
2. Decide whether sub-agent control-metadata-shaped replies (e.g. lines starting with `**Routing Kind:**`, `QUESTION_FOR_TASK_CREATOR`) should be stripped at the framework layer or fixed in the sub-agent reply contract.
3. Optionally cap SHELL grep timeout/scope in the planner template (or have it default to a narrower path than the monorepo root).

Happy to write the planner-prompt patch + tests for any of the above if there's preferred direction.

## Related

- #7923 (merged) — preserve speaker context + prevent Stage 1 phantom action claims
- #7924 (merged) — bound runaway tool-arg validation loops
- #7897 (merged) — restore evaluator messageToUser precedence
- #7931 (open) — drop regex-list response-handler evaluators (Stage 1 / response-handler layer; orthogonal to this issue)

topPRs.title: Restore packages/os/linux/variants/milady-tails — per Shaw'\''s go-ahead
topPRs.author: NubsCarson
topPRs.body:
## Authorization

Direct DM with @shawmakesmagic on 2026-05-24 ~05:57 PT:

> **shaw:** nah i think we should go with your thing
> **shaw:** that is what i was worried about
> **shaw:** we just need to have one version one build, the key thing is that both also support risc-v
> **shaw:** i think you were much farther along
> **shaw:** i am not attached to what my agents have done

This PR reverts `4c96fee98e` ("os/linux: consolidate to single elizaOS multi-arch build, remove Tails fork") and `58505f0cdd` ("chore(os/linux): remove stale milady-tails fragment per 4c96fee98e" — PR #7926, my mistake) by restoring the **byte-equivalent state** of `packages/os/linux/variants/milady-tails/` at 2026-05-21 02:59 PT (commit `4039bc612a`).

## What this restores

| | |
|---|---|
| Files | **6,157** total (6,105 vendored Tails + 52 nubs-authored customizations) |
| Vendored Tails | `variants/milady-tails/tails/` — exact snapshot of `gitlab.tails.boum.org/tails/tails` as it was the day before deletion |
| nubs customizations | Dockerfile, Justfile, PLAN.md, ROADMAP.md, README, 9 phase specs (rebrand → privacy-mode → bake-milady-app → autolaunch+agent → persistence → mode-parity-harness → customization-actions), brand assets (elizaOS_text_*, elizaos_logotext_*, logo_*), schemas (`model-catalog`, `update-manifest`), 13 scripts (`build-iso.sh`, `usb-write.sh`, `boot-qemu.sh`, `prepare-milady-app-overlay.mjs`, `generate-release-evidence.mjs`, `security-smoke.sh`, `sync-runtime-to-chroot.sh`, `validate-model-catalog.mjs`, `validate-runtime-overlay.mjs`, etc.) |

## What this gets back functionally

- **Tails greeter** — first-boot language / keyboard / passphrase UX (the wizard that wasn't reimplementable as a popup)
- **Tails `tps` Persistent Storage** — real LUKS-encrypted USB persistence
- **`persistence.conf` machinery** — live-boot kernel persistence activation
- Architecture per `docs/specs/phase-7-persistence.md`: *"no Tails persistence code is modified, only added configuration"* — i.e. **one `MiladyData` `Feature` subclass** added to Tails' `tps/configuration/features.py`, picked up automatically by `get_classes()`
- Full Tails security hardening (Tor, AppArmor enforcement, MAC randomization, amnesic tmpfs, hardening sysctls) without a partial re-implementation

## ⚠ Known gap — RISC-V

Tails has historically been amd64-only. **This subtree contains no riscv64 package lists or arch-specific hooks** (grepping the design docs for `riscv` returns zero hits). Shaw's stated key requirement is *"both also support risc-v"* — that gap is not addressed in this PR.

Two paths to consider (NOT in this PR — Shaw's call):

1. **Keep `packages/os/linux/elizaos/` as the riscv64-only path** while this Tails-based variant covers amd64 (and possibly arm64 via Tails ARM port if/when upstream lands). Violates Shaw's "one version one build" rule but is realistic short-term.
2. **Port Tails to riscv64** — touch Tails' `debian/control`, package lists, initramfs hooks, GRUB EFI setup; verify on QEMU `virt`; submit upstream or vendor diff. Real multi-week work, no upstream precedent.

## What this PR does NOT touch

- `packages/os/linux/elizaos/` is untouched. Both trees coexist after this lands. Shaw's "one version one build" decision is downstream of this PR.
- No changes outside `packages/os/linux/variants/milady-tails/`.
- The `MiladyData` `Feature` class is **design-only in the restored docs** (`phase-7-persistence.md` describes the implementation). If the actual Python landed before deletion and isn't in `4039bc612a`, a follow-up needs to port it from wherever it's now hiding.

## Verified

- 6,157 files restored exactly from `4039bc612a` (single `git checkout` command, no rewrites)
- `git diff --shortstat`: `6157 files changed, 1390540 insertions(+)`
- No file outside `packages/os/linux/variants/milady-tails/` modified

## License note

The vendored Tails tree under `variants/milady-tails/tails/` is **GPL-3.0-or-later**, as it was before. This subtree carrying GPL-3 code does not affect the licensing of `packages/os/linux/elizaos/` or the rest of `elizaOS/eliza`, since the variant is opt-in (built only when you target it). Shaw's prior consolidation cited GPL-3 derivation as the deletion reason; per his DM this is no longer the blocker.

topPRs.title: [WIP] First-party Satellites and dynamic view host bridge
topPRs.author: Dexploarer
topPRs.body:
## WIP Scope

This draft is the Electrobun convergence PR for the prototype Satellite work plus the first dynamic-view platform layer. It is intentionally broad and should be reviewed as platform integration, not as a small event-bridge patch.

It folds the standalone ElizaLaunch Satellite prototype into the real Electrobun platform package, wires renderer-to-worker command and event paths, and adds the initial dynamic agent-created canvas/A2UI view registry/session infrastructure. The production app UI remains untouched.

## Major Changes

### First-party Satellites moved into Electrobun

Adds first-party Satellites under:

`packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/`

Included Satellites:

- `eliza.runtime`
- `eliza.surface`
- `eliza.fs`
- `eliza.pty`
- `eliza.git`
- `eliza.local-model`

Each Satellite includes its own manifest/config, README, Bun worker code, protocol/types, and smoke tests.

### First-party Satellite seeding

Adds:

- `packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/first-party-satellites.ts`
- `packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/first-party-satellites.test.ts`

The registry marks:

- `eliza.runtime` as required
- `eliza.fs`, `eliza.pty`, `eliza.git`, and `eliza.local-model` as recommended
- `eliza.surface` as dev/admin only

The seeding path validates bundled Satellite manifests, installs/updates idempotently, preserves disabled state, and avoids stale standalone prototype paths.

### Runtime Satellite host-mode adapter

`eliza.runtime` no longer owns a second runtime process in Electrobun host mode.

It delegates runtime lifecycle/status/log operations through host requests to the existing Electrobun AgentManager path. Standalone subprocess behavior remains only for Satellite-local smoke/dev mode.

### Renderer-to-worker command bridge

Adds/uses the renderer-facing command path:

- `carrotInvokeWorker`

This lets dev/admin views and future dynamic views call `eliza.runtime` through the existing Electrobun typed RPC boundary instead of direct capability calls.

### Worker event tail bridge

Adds the renderer-facing event path:

- `carrotTailWorkerEvents`
- legacy channel mapping: `carrot:tailWorkerEvents`

Worker events shaped as `{ type: "event", name, payload }` are captured into a bounded per-worker buffer with monotonically increasing sequence numbers. Renderers can tail events by worker/Satellite ID and sequence cursor.

Defaults:

- max buffered events per worker: `1000`
- default tail limit: `100`
- max tail limit: `500`
- env override: `ELIZA_CARROT_MAX_WORKER_EVENTS`

This is the event substrate for dynamic agent-created views. It is not a live subscription system yet.

### Dynamic agent-created view infrastructure

Adds a host-side dynamic view layer under:

`packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/dynamic-views/`

Included pieces:

- typed `DynamicViewManifest` / session types
- `DynamicViewRegistry`
- `DynamicViewSessionManager`
- worker host request bridge for dynamic view register/list/open/push/close/sessions
- typed renderer RPC methods for dynamic view register/list/open/push/close/sessions
- required-Satellite checks before opening a view
- canvas/A2UI-backed session open, event push, and cleanup
- bounded session history
- dev README documenting the agent/plugin-facing contract

New typed renderer RPC methods:

- `dynamicViewRegister`
- `dynamicViewUnregister`
- `dynamicViewList`
- `dynamicViewOpen`
- `dynamicViewClose`
- `dynamicViewPush`
- `dynamicViewSessions`

New trusted worker host request methods:

- `dynamic-view-register`
- `dynamic-view-unregister`
- `dynamic-view-list`
- `dynamic-view-open`
- `dynamic-view-close`
- `dynamic-view-push`
- `dynamic-view-sessions`

Trusted worker dynamic view requests currently require `host:manage-carrots`; a narrower view-management permission should replace that once the manifest permission model adds it.

### Demo dynamic view

Adds a dev-only demo view:

- view id: `agent.run.trace.demo`
- file: `packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/dynamic-views/demo/agent-run-trace.html`

It demonstrates:

- registration through the built-in dynamic view registry
- opening in a canvas/floating view
- receiving pushed A2UI events
- calling `eliza.runtime` through `carrotInvokeWorker`
- tailing `eliza.runtime` events through `carrotTailWorkerEvents`

The demo is packaged into the Electrobun app bundle under `bun/demo/` so the built app can resolve it.

### Surface remains dev/admin only

`eliza.surface` remains an inspector/control harness.

The Surface Runtime client prefers:

1. `carrotInvokeWorker` for method calls
2. `carrotTailWorkerEvents` for events
3. existing bridge fallback
4. polling only as last fallback

Surface still calls `eliza.runtime` as broker and does not call capability Satellites directly.

### Capability Satellite summaries

`eliza.fs`

- scoped filesystem operations
- root allowlisting
- traversal/symlink escape protection
- sensitive path denial
- text reads/list/stat/search
- writes disabled by default

`eliza.pty`

- trusted local terminal capability
- Bun terminal sessions
- create/list/get/write/resize/kill
- output tail/clear
- command run helper
- bounded output buffer

`eliza.git`

- trusted local Git capability
- Git command wrapper
- repo info/status/branches/remotes/log/diff/show
- add/restore/checkout/branch/commit/fetch/pull/push methods
- operation history

`eliza.local-model`

- Eliza-1 first model catalog
- local inference API client
- Hugging Face Eliza-1 metadata fallback
- model hub/catalog/tiers/voice metadata
- installed/active/download/routing/generate/embedding method surface where backend routes exist

`eliza.runtime`

- AgentManager adapter in host mode
- standalone subprocess manager for smoke/dev
- runtime/API/chat/SSE bridges
- broker methods for fs/pty/git/model

### Build/package housekeeping

Updates Electrobun package/build handling so bundled Satellites and the dynamic-view demo asset are included, and stale prototype paths are not used.

Also adjusts macOS build icon handling so the committed `AppIcon.icns` path is used without depending on stale `.build` artifacts.

### Typecheck baseline repair

Fixes the Electrobun typecheck baseline by:

- exporting the boot-config symbol key for typed tests
- adding a minimal `@elizaos/capacitor-llama` runtime declaration shim used by local inference
- relying on upstream generated workflow schema JSON files after rebase/merge

## What This PR Does Not Do

- Does not add `eliza.voice`
- Does not add `eliza.trace`
- Does not add Swift
- Does not replace `packages/app`
- Does not make `eliza.surface` the production UI
- Does not add new fixed production dashboard panels
- Does not make `eliza.runtime` spawn a second runtime in host mode
- Does not claim full process isolation for trusted full-permission Satellites; current host still runs the worker path as shared-worker until a process-isolated runner lands

## Validation

Ran after merging current `origin/develop` into the WIP branch:

- `bun run --cwd packages/electrobun-carrots typecheck`
- `bun run --cwd packages/electrobun-carrots test`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun typecheck`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun test`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun build`
- `git diff --check`

The Electrobun test suite now includes the dynamic view registry/session tests and worker host-request dispatch coverage.

Satellite validations run during this WIP branch:

- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/surface typecheck`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/surface build`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/surface smoke`
- Runtime Satellite build and Phase 1/2/3 smokes using safe standalone command mode
- FS typecheck/build/smoke
- PTY typecheck/build/smoke
- Git typecheck/build/smoke
- Local Model typecheck/build/smoke
- App icon presence in the built Electrobun bundle
- dynamic-view demo asset presence in the built Electrobun bundle
- no leftover smoke/dev processes after cleanup

Build notes:

- `bun install --ignore-scripts` tempdir permission errors appeared during desktop build and were tolerated by the existing build script.
- Vite/Rolldown emitted existing `@electric-sql/pglite` direct `eval` and ineffective dynamic import warnings.

## Review Notes

This should be reviewed as a WIP platform convergence PR.

The main review surfaces are:

- first-party Satellite seeding and package inclusion
- host request path for AgentManager-backed runtime behavior
- `carrotInvokeWorker` and `carrotTailWorkerEvents` typed RPC surfaces
- bounded event buffering semantics
- dynamic view manifest/session lifecycle and canvas/A2UI reuse
- trusted worker host-request access to dynamic views
- Surface staying dev/admin only
- whether the first-party Satellite manifests/docs honestly communicate trusted capability boundaries and current shared-worker host behavior

The intended next phase is an agent-run/tool/subagent trace layer that opens dynamic views automatically, not another fixed dashboard panel.
